references.title: A Review of Self-regulated Learning: Six Models and Four Directions for Research

annotations.text: The paper discusses 6 of the most well known models of SRL. They present differences in history, description of the model, empirical support and available instruments that stem from differences in the model. The authors perform a meta analysis over the different models and draw reccomendations for practice. They outline that SRL is a coherent framework for research in educational psychology and call for researchers and educators to draw from the current knowledge in SRL to enhance student’s learning and SRL skills.

SRL has made substantial contributions to education psychology since its inception. One of the key conquests was the discrimination between SRL and metacognition. With time, the number of SRL models has been increasing and, in 2011, a handbook outlining the established methods of SRL was published. The authors propose to review the, at the time, existing knowledge on SRL.

The criteria for inclusion in the work were only established SRL models that have been previously published and cited in peer reviewed papers. Models that did not fulfill these conditions were dismissed. The authors kept the Zimmerman, Boekaerts and Winne that were assumed to still be active - for example Borkowski’s model have not been considered.

Other models that have been used after 2001 were also considered for the literature, thus bringing the models used to be those by: Zimmerman, Pintrich, Boekarts, Winne, Hokfides and, finally, the model by Hadwin, Jarvela and Miller.

Zimmerman developed 3 distinct SRL models and started his approach using socio-cognitive theory: people acquire knowlesge from socialization and observation of others. The initial models was known as a Triadic Analysis of SRL that analyzed 3 forms of SRL - environment, behavior and personal level.

The second model by Zimmerman focused in the Cyclical Phases of SRL explains the interrelation of metacognitive and motivational processes. This model was later tweaked in 2009 with more metacognitive and volitional strategies.
The third model represents the four stages in which students acquire self-regulatory competency.
Zimmerman uses 3 stages:

The cyclical model has been extensively tested in studies, both by Zimmerman and other authors. A study testing athletic skill profficiency between experts, non-experts and novices in multiple sports showcases that SRL skills play a role in the behaviour that is exhibited. In one example using basketball, experts performed more SRL related actions in comparison to others. In another example of Volleyball, SRL skills accounted for more than 90% of the explained variance in serving skills. Also in academic skills, higher achieving students exhibited an more SRL subprocesses than their colleagues. Keeping logs and diaries over progress has also been shown to positively affect SRL skills and performance.

Zimmerman’s model measures subprocesses that are partly based from the Self-Regulated Learning Interview Schedule. Moreover, the author has also developed his own methods to assess SRL in experimental settings.  Another method was later developed by Magno using the Academic Self-Regulation Scale which has validated using correlation analysis with the results obtained from other models - more specifically the MSLQ and the Learning and Study Strategies Inventory.

The work by this author is more focused on the role of setting goals and the motivation of students. The author has developed 2 distinct models of SRL. The first model was a structural model divided into six components:

The second model was evolved into what is called the Dual-Processing Self-Regulation Model. This model draws from multiplical psychology frameworks including motivation, emotion, metacognition, self-concept and learning. This model distinguishes different purposes of self-regulation during the learning process:

According to the Boekarts model, students will be prone to adopt strategies that align with their emotional state (motivation). Students who see a task as threathning will shy away from it in order to maximize their well-being. On the other hand, if the tasks align with the student’s goals and needs, they will actively look to pursue development and acquire knowledge on their path to mastery.

The authors identified how cognitive appraisals determine emotion and learning intentions and, for example, found that different genders receive, emotionally, the appraisals, resulting into different responses. These results were validated in other studies, showing that, for example, boys and girls attend differently to some types of problems such as math: boys tend to be more positive and more willing to put the efforts whilst girls were less prone to engage. In addition, the authors showed that the interpretation of what is meant to be done in a learning activity are better explainers of learning intentions than other domain related measures such as ability or interest in the domain.

Success in mathematics homeworks under this model was also shown to be positively influence by value appraisals. In addition, the author managed to create neural networks (using 56 predictors) that achieved an accuracy ranging between 94 to 100% accuracy.
Online Measurement Questionnaire - measuring sensitivity to learn in concrete situations
Interactive Learning Group System
Confidence and Doubt Scale
Use of Neural Networks to measure SRL

Winne and Hadwins model has been very popular in the implementation of computer supported learning settings as it mostly relied on the measurement of metacognitive strategies and their impacts on motivation.
The creators of this model believe that internal feedback is of paramount importance to SRL. The model presented by the authors occurs in 4 distinct phases:

While there is strong empyrical evidence for their work, there are some challenges lying in front of this model: the first is being more explicit on how phase 4 works - what processes are undertaken and what do the authors refer to as “long-term changes in their behavior”. Second - the model does not incorporate motivation. Thirdly, a discussion of how SRL evolves over the lifespan and finally, what is the impact of learning disabilities in SRL. More recent work has focused on applications of Data Mining and learning analytics to measure SRL. Up to this point, Winne’s model has been the only one explicitly focused on measurements of SRL using Trace data.

Pintrich is one of the main fathers of SRL research as his theoretical framework, analytical tools (MSLQ) and empyrical studies have, in large proportion, significantly contributed to the field. The Pintrich model follows 4 distinct phases:

The Pintrich conceptual model includes metacognitive research such as perceptions of learning and of knowledge acquisition. Moreover, his work also shows that both regulation and affection can be regulated and argues that individuals can make attempts to control their own overt behavior. The final component is regulation of the learning context.

No direct evidence concerning empyrical validation of the model, but Pintrich has made multiple contributions to the SRL field. MSLQ is the most widely used instrument when it comes to the measurement of SRL or self-efficacy.

Efklides has one of the strongest metacognition focus in comparison to other models (with the exception of Winne and Hadwin’s models). However, Efklides also puts a strong emphasis on motivation and affect. Despite it being fairly young (2011), the model has contributed to SRL research in great ways. The advantage of this model is that it builds upon existing SRL frameworks while also adding a connection with other metacognitive models.
The model has 2 levels:

This model seems to be focused in describing how SRL comes into play in the context of collaborative work. The model draws from Winne and Hadwin’s model and tries to complement it with the introduction of other SRL constructs in a group context.
